pages.text:
S, E, J & screaming C.  He, of course, won, pushing them in.  A leisurely afternoon swimming + boating + reading.  A perfect site
__July 22nd__
	Today we actually made it to Conques looked round the shops + church + had lunch in a friendly restaurant.  A woman was playing the piano in the church — rehearsing for a recital that evening.  It was beautiful.  The tympanum is awe-inspiring + very well preserved  it was too hot to walk down to see the old pilgrim's bridge, but we did look at the treasures + find out about St. Foy.
	After we arrived back at camp, the boys had a pedalo fight with the Dutch, + in the evening a treasure hunt was organised
__July 23rd__
	Rain poured early on, then the day warmed up.  John took himself down to the pool to swim — fortunately with

armbands.
	In the evening we went to Hotel du Nord at Montealey.  It was described in the book as "cosy".  Indeed, it was, + relaxing + good food.  Stuffed cabbage, veal + lovely puddings.  Myrtle pie / chestnut + pistachio icecream.  We also had a local dish — mashed potato, stretchy cheese + garlic.

__July 24th__
	It's cool + rainy today.  We set off early on a day trip to see Estaing and Espalion .  Lovely old houses, chateaux + churches.  In the evening the children played ping pong + other ball games with other children at the camp.  The weather warmed up in the evening.

__July 25th__
	We woke up at 6.45. + left at 7.45 for the canoe base on the river Lot.

We ate breakfast in the carpark before Simon, Thomas, William + Peter set off at 9.10. in 2 double Kayaks, or rather in a van taking them all 5 miles up-river to Entraygues to shoot the rapids down to here.

	It is pouring rain now - Eve is in the van with John + Charles. The Kayakists haven't bothered to take protection from the sun, or much to eat or drink because it is a far less demanding trip than last years in the Ardèche. The gorge is less steep, more inhabited, there are lots of green trees for shade +, from the maps the rapids look less tricky.

	The trip was good, but cold. At one point they had to get out + run on the spot to warm up.

	We had lunch at Hotel de la Vallée... The rest of the afternoon we spent

at the campsite.
__July 26th__
	We set off northwards in the rain. We went through the mountains, over the pass, Pas de Peyrol [?Pas], 5000 feet, where Thomas bought an enormous cow's horn in the shop at the top of the pass. But because of the rain + mist, we could see no scenery. On the other side, the weather was slightly better. The barns are not as pretty. We had lunch on waste ground in a village where Peter fell off a pile of logs.

	We camped in Viele Compte, a municipal campsite with an indoor municipal swimming pool * * *(*). Simon cooked garlic spaghetti. The boys had to wear swimming hats.
__July 27th__
	We set off northeastwards, towards Roanne, to interview an au pair,

haure Picasso. Her family gave us juice + cakes + biscuits in a lovely garden. All her brothers + sisters played with the boys. We all enjoyed it.
	We went on to Laconduière to camp; it has a pretty out[door] swimming pool + good swings for the children.
__July 25th__ We left before breakfast (7.45. Simon made tea, we got up at 8 + left at 9). because we had run out of bread. We had breakfast in the town square, + then went to church at 10. A cheerful church, a woman led the singing. We left just before the ~~creed~~sermon; + made less noise - apart from Charles screaming about his goorage, because we had sat in the back row.
	We set off on the old Paris-Nice road + had coffee in a lay-by.
	We motored a long way. Simon [to...]

captions: Flying buttress Beauvais (truncated because of Charles!) [illegible]
pages.text:
Flying buttress
Beauvais
(truncated because of Charles!)
[illegible]

his map – read twice outside Paris.
The campsite was Torcy. E. of Paris. very pretty.
Some old people obviously live there &
tend their gardens all summer. But
to our the wash-house smelt. It's an
old orchard.
__July 29th__
    After more trials on the Paris
motorway system, we reached Beauvais.
The church choir was closed for lunch, so
we bought patisserie & lunched on
the grass next to the church. The church
is amazingly high – like our new convention
centre.
    In the afternoon we stopped at
Crécy & stood on the site of King Edward's
windmill & looked down the hill where
English longbowmen had wiped out the
flower of French [?Chivalry/Cavalry].
    That evening we camped at
Beauvainville. We found it pretty,

near a stream, flat, crowded, trees.
Twenty-six children were playing at
the swings next to our van. The
children liked the swings. Too bad
there was no swimming. And because
it was Monday there were no restaurants
open. Perhaps we should take our
own ping pong bats & balls to France
another year.
    __July 30th__.
        We stopped at a supermarket
& caught the ferry. The children watched
cartoons.
    We drove home in the rain.
A very good holiday.

Mummy & car, my lily, John ate "rabbits"
for breakfast

102 August 4th.
    We drove down to Glouces[ter]
to drop Peter off at music camp, putting
up his tent. Then dropped off Simon's
'cello to be mended

August 10th.
    Kate Warburton has bravely come
with us. There were huge jams on
the M5. Kate babysat John, Charles &
William while Simon, Eve & Thomas
heard Peter sing & play his violin in the
orchestra at Beauchamp House.
    We picked up Simon's 'cello, &
drove on to __St. Augustine's__ working farm
__Arlingham__.
    Peter & John slept in the [?bukta] &
Kate slept on the bottom bunk. There
was a playground with an old tractor
in it for the boys to climb on, & a half-

pages.sketch_desc: Small hand-drawn sketch of a river loop labelled 'R. Severn' with an arrow showing the river winding around a loop, illustrating the text 'The River Severn has scoured a great loop around the village & fields.'
pages.text:
Chinese pig & hoardes of rabbits &
other animals.
Aug 11th. The next day we went for a
walk 3 miles, instead of church,
because the service wasn't until 11.
    The River Severn has
    scoured a great loop
    around the village & fields.
After lunch we dropped Thomas off
at __Beauchamp__ & set of for B'ham.

August 18th.
    Eve drove 5 boys & [Tony]
nanny to
Thorpeness. We arrived & had a
terrible time putting up the tent.
__John's birthday__
    John zigzagged up the
theare in a row boat with Tony
got tossed about in the sea in his

new swim suit from Grandma, rescued by Sally, watched to [?see] the floats in Aldeburgh Carnival + had his birthday tea back at Seamark.
	We stayed at Thorpeness for a week. Good sunshine. All went well until the Friday when very strong winds pulled the large tent down.

__August 31st.__
	We went to [?Trimley] for one night, William cooked a Pavlova. Thomas seranaded us with his violin.
Sept 7th We went to Jane + Harry's wedding anniv[ersary] party, camped at Frensham + had lunch with [?Jeffreys]
Sept 22nd
	We went to Kate + Jamie's farm for one night, picked plums + swam. Laura, the new au pair came too.

Sept 24th
	Simon + Eve go to Charles Maxwell's memorial service + Simon spends the night selling lamps in London.
	In [October]
November Eve took Thomas, William + Peter to Disneyworld, so we did not go camping at half-term.

April 3rd 1992.
    Simon, Eve, Thomas, William, Peter
John & Charles set off after lunch to the
camping shop at Wolverhampton. We bought
a new Igloo Tent, Korean-designed
knuckle joints - which only takes "2 minutes
to erect.
    An array of problems – the
bracket below a water tank has
broken – there has been 2 lots of
clanking – the Quinto campsite wouldn't
have us because we've not members.
The next campsite was too small.
We have ended up at __Ellesmere__ __Tetchill__, where
Eve has broken the corkscrew &
the van heater doesn't work. Ice
formed in the night & most of us
were cold.
    Sunshine at the moment, inspite
of gloomy weather for days. The campsite
is expensive (£10) a key for the loos

+ laundry – v. clean. Well laid out in
a little grassy field right in the
middle of town. Ellesmere College is
right on the doorstep.

→ April 4th.
    This campsite is so good that
I am upping it to *****. It is
clean + right near town. Walking to
shops & restaurants is easy. It's good
for the children to play football.
    Yesterday we went to the "Cellar"
for coffee – very welcoming. After
lunch we set off in the van & had
a look at Ellesmere College – a
Woodard school, & pleasant. We
walked back along the canal to
the Ellesmere town playground
which the children enjoyed. Eve
went of shopping on her own.

The "Cllers" could not have us for supper, so we went to an Indian Tandoori restaurant. Delicious food. We all wrapped up warm for the night – closing vents, wearing hats. Eve wore her waistcoat upsidedown, legs through armholes, & kept her aching hips warm. We all slept better.
      Church at 10.15., a walk up the road.
      Thomas practised his violin, (because he has an exam next week) with his music propped up inside the engine. At the end of the piece I said "Well done", but there was a sound of clapping behind the hedge, & 2 women complimented him on his playing.
      Church was very friendly, a sermon on the challenge of being disciples of Jesus – a cross in the box allusion to the coming general election.

On the way home we had lunch at Cloverley Hall, Whitchurch. V. pleasant. Evangelical. Where do we plan the family weekend away for St. George's.
      We got home at 2.15. on the Sunday. Charles is having to learn to take it in turns when sitting in the front seat.

April 21st.  →__Beauchamp House__ for Simon & Peter to join in a strings course. They are both enjoying it. Simon particularly the quartet work.
      There is a family called Yates – father & 2 children – from Park Hill Rd, Harborne, parked next to us.
      Eve arrived later in the morning with William, John & Charles, after dropping Thomas at Trimpley.
      It's quite cold, but pleasant sitting

outside, especially when the sun shines.
	We stayed until October 23rd. Simon + Peter enjoyed the course, Simon like Gulliver, the only adult among 85 children, singing an octave below the others. William, John, Charles & Eve visited the Simions in the Espace & bought antiques at Gloucester Docks.

May 23rd Sat
	A long drive to __Dinas__ - 12.15-5.20 V. slow on the motorways in the Midlands. In the end, we went right through the middle of Wolverhampton, & by-roads around Shrewsbury.
	We got supper ready & had just started when it rained, carried it into the tent, carried it out for pudding, when it started to rain again! But v. warm.

Sun 24th May
	Eve went up the hill for a walk & slither with Charles, rescued by Thomas.

We all sat around doing Maths, English & French homework. The 3 boys have to do 45 min. twice a day. John can almost read Red Hen & Sly Fox by himself.
	Church at 11.30. at Talybont.

From the beach near Llanbedr.

We had lunch sitting at the roadside on the grass. Charles is now just old enough to do this safely.
	We went to the beach at Lland[?]g. The [Cttis?] Church is the earliest in Britain, c.650. Built for St. Patrick's mission at Wales. The church, with our church, were having a barbque.
	Men were riding noisy motorbikes in the waves. John & Charles bought buckets & spades & Peter bought a water-pistol. We sat on damp sand & had an icecream. Lovely shells.
	When we got back to the campsite, Imogen was there with Tom & Caroline.
	Charles tried sleeping in the tent, but woke up & cried. So he's sleeping in the van, but John is sleeping in the tent.
__Mon 25th May__
	We all climbed into bed for Peter to open his presents. After 45 minutes homework we all went off on a tricky walk – 2 miles of bog[?]

After we returned the Hudfords arrived in time for Peter's birthday cake. The children play on the rope swing. 9 of us set off through the wood & across the fields for 8 pints of milk. 1½ hours later we returned with only one pint because the dairy is being painted. Later the Hudfords drove Thomas & Simion for some.
	We had a lovely leisurely barbeque. The Hudfords had just bought a boat.
__Tues 26th May__
	Very hot again as soon as we wake up. The boys set off to play on the rope swing. After more homework, Simon, Eve, Thomas, William & Peter set off on a walk, leaving Laura to look after John & Charles for the day.
	It was very, very hot. William in a foul mood – heaving rocks & thrashing bracken. Finally Simon allowed him to walk home on his own. We saw him from a great distance high up – bright yellow – striding

enthusiastically back towards Dinas.
	Afterwards we continued the walk + Eve got cross + said she wasn't enjoying it – scrambling uphill on all 4s. 500 ft in one mile. So we abandon the plan to reach the tops of Rhinog + just walk through the saddle between the hills. Perhaps more walking will have to wait until we all have boots rather than open sandals.
	We found shepherds herding sheep on our way down, + ~~[?derle]~~ deserted farm buildings. Simon, Thomas + Peter frolicked in the river when we reached the valley bottom, while Eve lay collapsed on the turf.
	We had another barbque that evening
	At 9.30. the wind picked up + Simon wondered whether the tent would hold, because ropes had snapped at Thorpeness last summer.
	After hours of worrying, Eve brought John into the van to join Charles + Peter, while

Simon tested the pegs. Then half an hour later at 2.30. we brought in Thomas + William while Simon collapsed the tent. Laura's dome spider tent was fine. John + Charles seemed to take those manoeuvres in their stride.
	We were all tired the next morning. Nevertheless

__Wed 27th May__
	We set off for Harlech + spent the morning there going up + down the tower before setting off for home. We had lunch at Trawsfynydd, a damned lake. Thomas had bought a frisbee + Peter, glasses.
	Then we stopped at Llanfair for Simon + [?+Laura] the boys to go on the train. It left at 4.15. We happened to meet up with the Bradishes. Eve met them at

Welshpool. We drove on home. We had had a marvellous, sunburnt holiday, one of the best ever. But on the Friday + Saturday following it has poured rain.

May 31st Sunday
     We Jill set off with Kate Warburton to Charlotte Maxwell's christening. John arrived + took Kate back to London. The Christening was at __Little Gaddesden__, + the reception/lunch was at Mrs. della Casa's house. It was a lovely warm sunny day, with 40 children. We gave Charlotte her father's school tuck box. David Maxwell came with Imogen + Iona, but Maxine was at another christening in London.
June 9th
     Simon, Eve, Thomas, Charles (chickenpox) went over to Malvern for Thomas to meet Boyle + Urbanchick, + all to meet the Hills. A good, interesting day.

Friday June 12.
     The 7 of us set off to see __Lincoln__. (Laura stayed at home to look after a French tennis player.)
     We left at 4.50., + got to Hartsholme Country Park ****, 2 miles outside Lincoln at 7.15.
     Simon put the tent up; Eve got supper ready, + the children disappeared off to the swings. Peter lost his new "cool hat + sunglasses. A lovely sunny evening.
     Hartsholme Park is the beautiful grounds of an old house, lake, bridge (where the hard rain broken in the night), + good playground. Just up the road is a school with swimming pool.
     On the Saturday we caught a bus into Lincoln which dropped us south of the old south gate on the main pedestrian shopping road. John + Charles had a ride on a roundabout. It was incredibly gay

& mediaeval-looking, with all the people & bustle & bright colours, groups of people stopping to chat & buskers. We walked through the south gate, Stonebow, early C16, originally the Roman gate. Further up the [?Steep] hill is the Jews House, very pretty.
      The Cathedral is being cleaned. It's very beautiful. 1072-[?12], homogenous architecture, the third largest in England. (We didn't have time to visit the nearby castle 1065) There is a quiet adult chapel for private prayer, & also a prayer corner where you can light candles, put them in sand in tris [a more friendly place for children]. Thomas & Peter paid their money & there were no candles left, so they had to ask a flunky for more. Also there are 4½ ft. high candlesticks, made at the limit of the potter's wheel & firing. One of these had barbed wire around it.

An earthquake split the original cathedral & St. Hugh, a Carthusian monk from Avalon & Burgundy, rebuilt it. "He was a man of great energy, powerful mind & inflexible purpose."
 It was built right through the original Roman wall. It is recorded that when Hugh died, the Jews wept at his funeral. His shrine was finished by 1280.
      There is also a huge bronze statue of Tennyson 1809-92 by G.F. Watts on the Chapter house green.
      We had lunch at Pimento – good stuffed peppers, & caught the bus back to the van. After tea we all went swimming, even Charles, who was only just over his chickenpox.
      At 9.30. on the Sunday we reached the Cathedral for the main Sunday service. Unfortunately, the choir was away. We were squashed

pages.sketch_desc: Hand-drawn map in the lower half of the page showing the area around Lincoln. Labels include: R. Trent (left/west side), Fossedyke Roman Canal (running roughly east-west), Braypool (a settlement on or near the canal), Lincoln (labelled with an arrow pointing to it), Lincoln Gap (right/east side), R. Witham (running south-east from Lincoln), R. Wibblen (a river label to the south/south-west), Fosse Way Bath to Humber (bottom-left, indicating the Roman road alignment), and 'escarpment from Cotswolds, Samestone' (bottom-centre/right). A partially visible label at the far right edge reads '[?Todston]' — possibly Torksey or Todeston. Lines and arrows indicate the course of rivers, the canal and the ridge/escarpment.
pages.text:
on a bench at the front, although there were 7 empty pews. A sermon on the Trinity.
	We returned to the campsite when the children reluctantly did their homework & wrote letters. Charles lost his purse at the swings.
	We set off for home past the old Roman canal, linking Lincoln with the R. Trent. (The land needs drainage ditches.) "Llyn" is Celtic for pool. "Coln" comes from the Roman Colonia.

[Route sketch labels:]
R. Trent
Fossedyke
Roman Canal
Braypool
Lincoln
Lincoln Gap
R. Witham
R. Wibblen
Fosse Way Bath to Humber
escarpment from Cotswolds, Samestone
[?Todston]

We stopped in at a camping stop near Lichfield. There were several electric power stations on the River Trent, each with a hill of coal brought by barge from the Yorkshire & Nottinghamshire coal fields.

July 19th Sunday
	We all left at 9.30. for Portsmouth then had to go back home from Selly Oak for Simon to switch off Thomas' keyboard & other electrics.
	We had lunch in the queues on the quayside, & had to keep interrupting to move up. On the boat we paid to see Hook, then had a delicious, eat-as-much-as-you-want meal by P&O for £50.
	Once off the boat we motored for ½ - 1 hr. to a small campsite in a village, ending up in the dark by mistake in the residential end. ** Pont-Audemer

July 20th
	We set off & found a prettier campsite

in a field for our way back. We'll send the address to Laura, & her father might drive her there to join us the day before the ferry leaves.

	We motored a lot of the day & the boys fought furiously. We had lunch at Abbaye de la Trappe a monastery.

	In the evening we arrived at __Ruilly__ ****. A lovely, cool river to swim in. V. humid. Clean loos. William & Peter weren't allowed to swim. We had a good anniversary meal at St. Pierre, along to road in the village. No choice, just plonked in front of you, so much easier with the children. John had pooed twice on the ferry, & Charles x3, so we're trying to cut down on their food.

	In the night there was a fabulous thunderstorm. It looked like the sky was going to be blasted, lifted right of.

Simon went out to check William & John in the tent, getting soaked. Then Eve sent him out again to be in the tent during the storm. A branch broke off the willow tree above us, but fortunately got caught & didn't fall.

	Many trees were snapped off in the night. We are driving through a very fertile countryside, apples, caves, beautiful, honey-coloured buildings, & Cotswolds, v. soft stone, caves. We cross the R. Loire on a long suspension bridge, supported on 4 towers. Charles screams when he has to leave the front seat. He has not yet understood the rota system. He's using words like "wide" river, "actually".

__Tuesday July 21st__.

	We drove to Azay-le-Rideau William & the other boys liked the tapestry of the judgement of Solomon. There is a lovely facade & bas relief, light & feathery.

pages.sketch_desc: Small hand-drawn sketch at the bottom of the page depicting a Mississippi-style riverboat with paddle wheel, funnels and a flag.
pages.text:
Afterwards we had crêpes.
	Then we drove on for 2 nights at __Richilieu__ ***
	__Wednesday July 22nd.__
	We went out towards Chinon. On the way we passed a beautiful old chapel __Champigny sur Veude__ 1499 lovely. proportions Sainte Chapelle, Stained glass windows, + marble statue of kneeling duke.
	On to Chinon. We parked at the top of the ridge + walked down into the old town with narrow streets. We saw a wine-making/coopering museum, animated models + English recorded commentary
	We had a good chicken lunch, but it was rather slow, so we went just down the road for icecreams. Chinon had a superb-looking Mississippi riverboat

In the afternoon we climbed the hill back to the top to look at the long castle. There was a loo in the castle, + a huge, flat space at the top with trees growing. Several towers: in one the tower was a puppet show — very very good. One man puppeteer on a wagon in a room. Blue celophane shook as the sea, a sleeping monk fell out of a sail boat + drowned + his soul was carried up to God by a knight on a winged horse. We saw a king + queen watching court musicians + a juggler + a tournament. Joan of Arc recognised the Dauphin among his courtiers. Finally a giant — 2 legs — fell asleep with his feet over the castle walls.
	We drove back to Richilieu + swam. Eve + William washed clothes.
	__Thu July 23rd__
	We walked around Richilieu — lovely city walls, still lived in in higgledy piggledy houses + lush gardens in the old moat. There's a beautiful covered market.

At 12. we set off south & lunch in a beautiful spot by a river
William & Thomas fought & William was kicked in the thigh & had a mega tantrum.
	Too hot in the afternoon, after checking 2 campsites on the way we finally got to __St. Estephe__ ** & swam. It was rather crowded, & the loos were poorly lit & not clean in the evening especially. There was a lake, but we had to motorrow it to reach the beach.

__Fri July 23rd.__
	It rained as we ~~set~~ got up, so we sat in the van, reading. We went to Piégut to shop, & returned to the lake beach to make lunch. In the afternoon we swam & hired pedaloes. We spent a couple of hours in the camp & then went out to a restaurant in Piégut - the boys had pigeon & we, Simon & I, had duck.

We usually order 5 helpings & divide them up among us. ~~When we~~ We started supper at [?10] 8.05. & left about 9.45. The boys were good & in bed by 10.30.

__Sat July 24th__
	The day looked brighter. We set off south & stopped at __Brantôme__. Monks had founded a monastery in the cliffs with a special spring. They eventually built an L shaped bridge to reach the village. The church had a lovely [?C] bas relief, small, over the font, of smiling figures - St. John Baptising Jesus, Jesus' robe held by an angel. We looked at the ruins of the cliffs.
	The town is beautiful today - balustraded walks along the wide river, trout old houses. The monks had left behind a tall bell tower with large [?L] gables against the triangular roof.
	We had lunch in an overgrown meadow next to the river. Then we drove on

to the cave of __Rouffignac__. We bookd tickets on a train half-an-hour later, & dashed back up the hill to the van for a cup of tea & patisserie.

    The caves were wonderful, 'grabbing', scraped drawings of mammoths, or black charcoal. Also modern grafiti as old as 1760. Sea caves. We had a good guide. The trip took an hour 5.15-6.15, on the way back Charles was bursting for a pee, so Thomas & I had to help him lean perilously off the train for a [?].

    We looked at 3 campsites near __Les Eyzies__, zigzagging round the hills, before finding a lovely farm "Le Queylou" ★★★★  run by a Dutchman. A superb view of green hillside & valley & one red-tiled roof. Modern facilities at one end of an old barn. At the other end were picnic tables & a ping pong table.

__Sunday July 25__

    We set off quite late & got to church at __Tayac__ 20 minutes before the service at 11.00. The best church we'd been to in France. Full of people at all ages. Enthusiasm, awareness. The church is partly carved into, built into the hillside. Simon & Thomas stayed in [illegible] until the end of the sermon. Eve, as usual, went out after 20 minutes with the rest of the children. The priest spoke about needing to come to God & sunbatte in prayer.

    We had a good lunch in __Les Eyzies__ at the __Vieux Moulin__. There was a water wheel turning cogs inside the restaurant. Simon & Eve had the cheapest meal ~~£50~~ 85 francs each, the boys had 50 francs each. Simon got so fed up with Peter's & William's fight after church that he threatend to

leave them in residential summer camp for 3 weeks. They stopped fighting from 11.45 until 8. pm.
	After lunch we walked along the road to the Caves of the __Font de Gaume__, but there was a 3 day wait to get in. They only allow 375 people per day inside, & they've the best caves after Lascaux, which are now closed. We went on a 6 mile drive to a very crowded lake. Simon didn't like the look of what was floating in the water. Charles waded knee-deep out to a slide & shot down it into deep water! He did look indignant when his ring brought him up to the surface. John swims with one arm-band, so that I can see him.
	We returned to Les Eyzies to see the caving museum- Scrapers, statues

captions: Sketched by Eve while Simon in supermarket.
pages.text:
& skeletons.
	Back at the camp we had spaghetti for supper, because we'd been unable to buy bread. It was a beautiful evening.
	~~We~~ __Monday July 26th__
	We set off & stopped at __St. Cyprien__ to shop. (delicious [?boulang-] not selling pastries)

Sketched by Eve while Simon in supermarket.

Thomas is doing all the map reading, very well! We went to Daglan, Nr. Domme to see Monique, Eve's old tennis partner, but she was on holiday in Spain. We met M. Fayolle, her mother, very nice. She suggested that we go to Rocamadour to see the eagles.

      We had lunch in the middle of a small village, Fon[?], next to the stream. Then we drove on to __Rocamadour__. We caught a slanting lift down to see the old shrine. There is a black madonna, enthroned, with a regal child on her lap. Simon thought it a very beautiful statue, slightly off-centre. Hanging from the ceiling is a bell which rings when a miracle is about to happen. Both the statue & the bell are very old & date from before the first of the sackings of Rocamadour in about (13. Warring lords kept sacking Rocamadour because of all its pilgrim riches, including Plantagenet princes: these culminated in the protestants' sacking it & destroying

much of it. It was only revived as a pilgrimage centre 100 years ago.
	Soon after leaving Rocamadour we passed a campsite with swimming pool in a pretty field, & decided to stay there for the night. **/** "Camping le lot"
It had a small swimming pool & a lovely old stone barn. Other features which attracted the boys were the pingpong table, breadcroissant, badminton, playgrounds.
	In the morning we returned to __Rocamadour__ to watch the eagles. They have a remarkable birds sanctuary — everything from black kites which eat grasshoppers, vultures, owls, & eagles. The head man — a power maniac; stood us out in the hot sun & harangued us for ages on the ideals of his zoo — saving birds, pronature antipollution.... Finally the birds started flying. They were remarkable — they come when one of

the keepers raises his gloved arm. One fish-eater, skimmed across a pool to scoop up a fish. Others swooped in front of us from tower to tower. One bird was put on John's & Peter's heads. 2 [?cuts] eagles went off into the sky & refused to come back. They were still messing about when we left.
	On the way to __Casanourse__ **** we stopped to book in at Le Beau Sejour at Calvinet. They wanted us at 8 at the latest. People seem to eat earlier down here — there were already people eating when we walked in.
	When we reached the campsite we swam & Eve did a load of wash — the da 4 days of clothes, one load, for £2. Such a relief. The next day I put all the bed sheets & pyjamas in.
	At Calvinet we had our best meal yet. Eve had Charlotte-cold, subtle-tasting mold of cucumber & potato,

cold, sweet tomato & oil. Then I had poached egg on vinegared bread & goose liver cream. The main course was duckling in a dark brown sauce & a mould of round potatoes, crisp ones on top. For pud I had coffee, chocolates & caramel sauce.
	Back at the camp, Charles slept in the cab, Thomas outside, & William & Peter & John in the tent.

__Wednesday 28th July__
	We spent the day in camp. The bread van brought breakfast at 9.15. We swam & had a pedalo fight. Usually we have salami for breakfast, but we didn't that day because of such an enormous supper. We had salami & salad & plums for lunch. For tea we had rockcakes & for supper, cheese, tomato & yog.
	Thomas & John slept out, but Eve felt a drop of rain (was it rain?) in the middle of the night, & insisted on taking

Thomas & John into the tent. There were 14 cyclists, all on purple bikes, just down the hill, about 18 years old, having a super outing sleeping under the stars.

__Thursday 29th July__
	Set off for __Entraques__. One of the nicest towns we've been in. Old, & not much done-up. A calm, well-kept church, an old wooden gate with a door-knocker – a hand tapping on a forehead. Old stone/wood buildings, with over-hanging upper storeys. We had lunch in a restaurant near the river. We used the bank & post office as well as buying food.
	On the way home we booked 2 canoes for 9.15 am. Saturday.
	Lovely patisseries for supper.
	John & William slept outside.

__Friday 30th July__
	As usual delicious croissants from 9.15 bread van.
	We're spending the day swimming &

pedaloing. Simon is trying to sort out pocket money, while Eve writes post cards. The boys have made friends with a family from Leicester, Steve & Pauline, Dan, Ben & [?Jen-] [?Swanton] at school at Stoneygates
__Sat 31st July__
	We left early & had breakfast at the canoe depot. Simon, Thomas & William & Peter went canoeing, while John & Charles & Eve waited. John & Charles paddled while Eve read. They returned in a foul temper because Peter had refused to paddle when he wasn't with Daddy.
	We had lunch at Vielleville, & then returned, to spend the afternoon at Casanrouse.
__Sumer 1st August__
	It would have been better to leave at 9.30, but we only left at 10. because of waiting for croissants

from the bread van. Then we set off for Conques. We parked down in the valley belt below the little chapel. Last year we tried to walk down to it, but it was too hot. This year we started to walk up at 10.55.. It was padlocked, but the walk was marvellous. Old, old, village, steep pedestrian streets, water for horses to drink, central gutters down the streets, lush gardens. We arrived at the abbey at 11.10. Mass had started already. It was very cool at the back.
	We bought leather & wooden toys & had a rather stuffy lunch with special ice creams. We sat outside, but it would have been cooler in the dark interior. After lunch we looked at the cloisters & cemetery. They have built an amazingly high wall to make a flat place for the cemetery. We [booked]

tickets for the Haydon, Bartok Quartets on Monday evening. The restaurant was the first one which had not allowed us to use access. We are now short of cash, & the banks are closed on Monday.

__Monday 2nd August__
	The campsite 'owner' telephoned her boss, who telephoned someone else, so that we can pay by Travellers cheques. The Casaniouge bank only opens on Wednesday morning.
	We have spent the day swimming & washing clothes, & trying to get things dry after a shower of rain.
	He swam again with the Swantons & left for Conques at 6.0. We looked around the town, had supper & left William, Charles & John in the square, on the stone bench outside the Abbey church. The Benedictine monks were friendly & [?chatted]. We left 2 sleeping bags &

several books. [?to] The concert was marvellous in the floodlit nave. The programme was Mozart, Bartok & Schumann. Very beautiful.
	Afterwards we woke Peter up off Simon's lap & went & collect John & Charles, who were both asleep, & William. Simon carried Charles back to the van & strapped him onto the kitchen seat. John was very good & walked back holding Eve's hand & a piggy-back half-way. We drove about 3 miles north from Conques & turned off into a field & all slept in the van [?2.00] John slept on the floor & Eve could just use the loo in the night. There was a fantastic view on both sides from the field.

__Tues [?August]__
	We had breakfast in the van - the boys complained it was cold.

pages.sketch_desc: Outline map of France with a route line drawn from le thore / Vallée de la Seine in the north, down through Châteaux de la Loire and Bourges, then south-west to Périgord Caves, and south to Gorges de Truyères, Cassaniouze, and Conques. Year label '1992' appears to the right of the map.
pages.text:
We drove along winding roads through beautiful little villages, & arrived at Etraques, where it's market day, to shop. Then on up the valley of the Truyères, with hydro electric dams, & purpose-built 1930's villages.

[Route sketch of France, labelled 1992, with route line showing:] le thore / Vallée de la Seine / Châteaux de la Loire / Bourges / Périgord Caves / Gorges de Truyères / Cassaniouze / Conques

We had lunch in a very dirty wood & came on to a crowded, but pleasant campsite on the La Source* * * Lac du Sarrans, created by the Sarrans

pages.sketch_desc: Hand-drawn diagram of the swimming pool layout at the campsite, showing: chute on the left leading into the pool, 'deep' and 'swimming' sections, a bridge above, sun-warmed seat at top left, foot bath at top right, a 'shoe' area, '4 sprays into pool' entering from the right, 'steps down', and a 'chil's pool+slide' at bottom left. Also annotated 'eating' area outside the pool. Caption reads 'Pool in confined area at campsite.'
pages.text:
damn. There's a swimming pool where we all enjoyed swimming, & John grew more & more confident swimming out from the chute. We all had an ice cream afterwards to celebrate his swimming the width.

[Sketch of pool layout at campsite, labelled:] sun-warmed seat / bridge / foot bath / chute / deep / swimming / shoe / 4 sprays into pool / steps down / chil's pool+slide

Pool in confined area at campsite.     eating

Wash-rooms under tennis court.

__Wed August 5th__

      We wanted to leave early, but stayed for a swim. The pool only opened at 10.00.. We drove north [past] out of the Gorges & the Chestnut Trees. We spent hours on the A71, going past villages, castles & farm houses. We drove for 8 hours & ended up camping in quite a pretty campsite in Salbris * * *. The restaurant,

Hotel du Parc, was the best, most friendly of the holiday. I had pâté, fish (lieu-noir?) fromage-frais with chives + chocolate mousse pudding. The sign on paying in the lobby stated how much it was per personne, deux personne + chien R.C. 50fr. Anglian dogs obviously don't cross the channel.

__Thu August 6th__
		We chatted to a couple from London in the next tent who had been to the Olympics in Barcelona. They complained that ticket purchasing was not centralised.
		We set off towards Chartres + arrived at 12.50. Thomas + Eve spent an hour looking at stained-glass windows starting to devise a quiz for the B'ham cathedral choir trip in October.
		We then paid £13 for a snack lunch in a bar - jambon et fromage croissants, icecream + coffee.

After another 1½ hours' motoring we reached __St. Illiers la Ville__ ****+
jumped into the swimming pool which is perhaps rather heavily chlorinated.
(See P.80.).
		There is also a large trampoline which Peter particularly enjoys. Ten or so children sit around the edge of it, while each child in turn has a bounce in the middle, or else 5 or so children bounce up + down all at once. A P.E. instructor's nightmare. The trampoline is in a wood under tall trees in the shade, along with 2 ping pong tables.
		The site is only 5 minutes from the station, + the train run into Paris is 50 minutes, so it would be possible to do day trips to Paris or ~~De~~ Eurodisney from the site.
__Fri 6th August__
		We lazed about at the campsite enjoying the trampoline + swimming,

had lunch, + then set off to shop in Brownie + at a supermarket. We arrived at the campsite at 5.30. Laura + her father + Benjamin had already been here an hour, because we had said that we would arrive at 4. It is peaceful with an orchard + ping pong. Quite low key + old showers, but clean + friendly.
	Eve beat everyone at ping pong, except Thomas, who beat Eve 22-20.

__Sat 7th Aug__
	We got up at 5.3[0]15. + left at 6.15, without breakfast. We ate breakfast while mincing along in the traffic jams. On the boat we just had sandwiches + watched Sean Connery in "Medicine Man".
	After Southampton we called in on Hathie, but unfortunately they were all out. There were awful traffic jams. Finally we stopped + had tea in an almost

deserted Newbury station, where we picked hundreds of blackberries. After Newbury the traffic moved faster up to Birmingham.
	It was a fabulous holiday. Noone got sick. Thomas, William + Peter now have a rota for doing the washing up + drying up, while Eve reads aloud, which makes life much easier.
	Thomas most liked Cassanouse; William most liked the pedal boats + pedalo fights, + Peter most liked the first French restaurant we went to, St. Pierre, where we ate just what they put in front of us, + we did not choose from a menu.

__Sat 15th August__
	We set off at 10.35., Eve, Laura, Thomas, William, Peter, John + Charles, to spend the night at Annette's on the

way to Thorpeness. We had lunch in a field; Eve had bought tip-over yoghurts, & most of the children tipped the yoghurt over themselves while tipping the flavour into the yoghurt.
	At Annette's Eve made tea, & then we went for a walk with Penel & the 2 dogs, Lucy & Pippa. Very energetic, romping puppies. We met Charles Henneker on the way. His son, Penel's godson, Thomas, 9, was spending the night with Penel. Thomas slept with Penel & Thomas, while Laura, William & Peter slept in the house & John & Charles & Eve were in the van. It rained in the night, but was clear next day. It was quite a squash getting up onto the flat bit of Annette's drive. Thomas & Annette had to hold the tree branches back. Annette cooked us a very good barbeque – she had started the charcoal

in a jug.

__Sunday 16th August__
	Annette gave us breakfast & we went to church at Eye. Lovely high church. We had to return to Thornham Magna because Thomas had forgotten his cap.
	We reached __Thorpeness__ in time for lunch.
	Jane feeds us lunch & we have camping van breakfasts & suppers every day. I hope it doesn't rain.

__Sunday 24th August__
	__Friday__ Yesterday Simon joined us. Today he drove off at 10·15. to take Thomas to Beauchamp. Then Simon drove home to B'ham. Everyone else went home in the rela[illegible]

__Friday 29th August__
	Simon, Eve, [strikethrough: Laura], William, Peter, John, Charles went down for the Beauchamp

concert, William babysat John + Charles in the van while Simon, Eve, William + Peter went in to hear Thomas play in a Mozart quartet.
__Saturday 30th August__
	We went into Gloucester + looked around the antique warehouse + the walked right around the old docks at Gloucester, looking at the history + layout. After Thomas' orchestral concert (works by Thea Musgrave, Schubert's Unfinished Symphony, Barber of Seville by Rossini, Britten's Matinée Musicale), we drove home

__Saturday 5th September__
	We set off at 8.50. + had breakfast on the motorway services. Not a good idea — queues + queue-bargers. We arrived at the __Peak District__ + walked for 3 hours in __Goyt Valley__. Good weather, despite the forecast. People paragliding near a disused railway.
	At 3.30 we left for Buxton + walk[ed]

into the town. We saw the museum + art gallery, with models of prehistoric scenes. The Crescent is boarded up. The proportions are lovely, like Bath, but not so large + awe-inspiring. There is also a hospital with a dome + a hotel in the same style. The Pavillion, glassed-in, reminded us of Malvern. We saw the spring where the water is bottled.
	We reached the campsite at 6.0. King Sterndale, east of Buxton.
	We camped across the road from an old church which only had a service in the afternoon. The evening was fine, but it started to rain in the night. Siri, the new German au pair, moved into Thomas's + Peter's tent in the middle of the night, + Thomas obligingly moved out into hers.

On the Sunday Simon, Thomas, Eve & John attempted a walk, but the rain was coming across horizontally, so we gave up…. & all went home. Sir left 5 days later.

__Friday October 23rd. 1992__
   Simon, Eve, William, John & Charles set out for York. (Thomas & Peter singing with Birmingham Cathedral choir in Paris & Chartres.) We leave at 2.10. It's raining + there are dreadful, sudden jams on the M1 between Nottingham & Sheffield. We overshoot the turning off to York because it is not marked. Finally we reach __York__ almost via Wetherby.
   We end up on the __Rowntree Park__ Caravan Site, near the river just south

of the city.

__Saturday__
   We walk into York along the river past where the boat left us 3 years ago. We reach the __Jorvic Centre__ at 9.25., just before the crowd starts. It's a fabulous museum, & fully lived up to my expectations, I had long wanted to visit it. It's important to spend the night in York & get there early. We bought a big tapestry in the museum shop for us all to do.
   We had coffee, hot chocolate & croissants & then went on to the __Minster__. Soaring, simple, disciplined architecture, & a myriad of things to look at below. A Miner in a little hole in the wall (model) ent[illegible]
